critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-constructed — customer-level splits, consistent information horizons, preprocessing fit on train only, single test-set evaluation. Most of the obvious leakage and confound problems are explicitly addressed. However, I identify three genuine methodological concerns worth naming precisely.

**Issue 1: Customer-level random split breaks temporal causality in a panel setting**

The claim: splitting customers randomly across 24 months ensures comparable mixes of seasons and churn prevalence, and that temporal ordering is preserved within each customer.

Why this is wrong: The methodology mixes reference dates across calendar time within the training set. A customer with a reference date in month 2 and a customer with a reference date in month 22 both appear in training. The model trained on this mixture is then evaluated on test customers whose reference dates also span all 24 months. This is not temporal holdout — it is a cross-sectional holdout on a panel. The problem is not within-customer leakage (that is handled correctly); the problem is cross-customer temporal leakage. Specifically: events that are in the 'future' relative to early-panel customers are in the training features of later-panel customers. The transformer can implicitly learn patterns that only became stable in later data (e.g., post-promotion behavioral shifts, platform feature rollouts, macro-economic shocks) and those patterns will be equally available at test time because test customers also span the full 24 months. The model is never forced to generalize forward in calendar time. In production, the model will always be predicting on customers whose reference dates are strictly after any training data — a fundamentally different evaluation regime than what is tested here.

The stratified-by-quarter reporting mitigates observability of this issue but does not fix it. A proper temporal holdout (e.g., train on months 1–18, validate on 19–21, test on 22–24) would test the hypothesis the business actually cares about: does the model generalize forward in time?

Evidence for/against: Compare AUROC and ECE when the split is done temporally (last N months as test) versus the random customer split described. If the temporal split substantially degrades transformer performance more than the logistic baseline, this is the mechanism at work — the transformer is exploiting cross-time signal that the static baseline cannot.

**Issue 2: ECE as primary metric is poorly matched to the evaluation design and will be dominated by the majority class**

The claim: ECE over decile bins of predicted probability reflects whether scores support budget-aware intervention tiers.

Why this is wrong in this specific setup: ECE computed over uniform deciles of predicted probability in a churn-imbalanced dataset (churn is typically 5–20% in e-commerce) will be dominated by the low-probability bins where most customers cluster. A model that is perfectly calibrated for the 80–90% of non-churners but miscalibrated for the top-risk decile will show excellent ECE. The retention workflow described cares specifically about the top-k highest-risk customers (as evidenced by the Recall@k secondary metrics). Calibration of the top decile or top ventile is what matters operationally — global ECE will mask failures exactly where they are most costly. Furthermore, the transformer's richer output distribution may produce sharper predictions (higher confidence) that are systematically miscalibrated relative to the logistic baseline's better-regularized probability estimates. A transformer that ranks better (higher AUROC) but is overconfident in the high-risk tier could show worse ECE than logistic regression even when it is strictly more useful for retention. The methodology has no mechanism to separate these cases.

Evidence for/against: Report calibration curves and ECE computed separately for the top-k predicted-risk decile versus the rest. If ECE is dominated by low-risk mass, the global metric is uninformative for the stated use case. A reliability diagram broken by predicted-probability quartile would clarify this.

**Issue 3: The baseline is likely too weak for the claimed isolation of sequence-structure benefit**

The claim: logistic regression on 12-dimensional static aggregates isolates whether sequence structure drives gains beyond aggregated features.

Why this is structurally incomplete: The 12 static features include 'top product categories' and 'device type' which are categorical aggregates, and the methodology does not specify how these are encoded for logistic regression. If they are binary flags or frequency vectors of high cardinality, they could dominate the logistic model or be poorly regularized. More importantly, the comparison conflates two confounds: (a) the benefit of sequential modeling and (b) the benefit of a richer feature set + a more expressive model. A gradient-boosted tree on the same 12 static features, or logistic regression augmented with hand-crafted temporal features (e.g., purchase trend slope, inter-purchase interval variance, session frequency trajectory), would partially decouple model capacity from sequential inductive bias. If the transformer beats only a 12-feature logistic regression but not a 200-feature GBDT, the conclusion that 'sequence structure drives gains' is not supported — it is equally consistent with 'more features and a more expressive model drive gains.'

Evidence for/against: Add a GBDT baseline on an expanded static feature set including manually constructed trend and volatility features over the same 12-month window. If the transformer does not beat this baseline, the sequence-structure hypothesis is not isolated.

**Summary:** Issue 1 (temporal leakage via random cross-sectional split) is the most fundamental and affects the validity of any conclusion about production deployment. Issue 2 (ECE as primary metric misaligned with operational needs) affects interpretation of whether the winning model is actually better for the stated use case. Issue 3 (baseline too weak to isolate the mechanism) affects whether the hypothesis — that sequence structure specifically matters — can be confirmed.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis

### Critique 1: Random customer-level split breaks temporal causality in a panel setting

**Critic's claim:** By randomly assigning customers with reference dates spanning all 24 months to train/val/test, the methodology allows the model to learn patterns from 'future' calendar periods during training (because later-panel customers' features are in the training set). This means the model is never forced to generalize forward in time, whereas in production it always will be. Stratified-by-quarter reporting mitigates observability but does not fix the underlying regime mismatch.

**Assessment:** This is a valid and substantive concern. I will not fully concede that the design is wrong for all purposes, but I must acknowledge a genuine gap between what is being evaluated and what the business ultimately needs to know.

The design intent was to maximize statistical power and comparability across segments by ensuring every split has a representative mix of seasons, tenure, and churn prevalence. That intent is legitimate: a purely temporal split (e.g., last 6 months as test) risks conflating calendar-period distribution shift with model generalization, and makes it hard to say whether a performance drop reflects a worse model or a different population. Customer-level random splits are a well-established practice in recommendation and churn literature precisely for this reason.

However, the critic is correct that this creates a regime mismatch with production deployment. The trained model will never see future-period data during live scoring — it will always predict forward. If the transformer learned behavioral patterns that only stabilized in months 18–24 (e.g., post-promotion behavioral signatures, platform feature rollout effects), those patterns are available to it at test time under the random split but would not be available to a model trained purely on months 1–18. This is not within-customer leakage, but it is a form of cross-temporal optimism.

The stratified AUROC by quarter does partially address this: if the model performs dramatically better on later quarters, that is a signal. But the critic is right that this is observational mitigation, not structural prevention.

**What I can defend:** For the stated goal of answering 'does sequence modeling outperform static features under matched conditions with comparable populations,' the random split is defensible — it is the fairest comparison between the two model families. Both models benefit equally from the cross-temporal mixing, so the relative comparison (transformer vs. logistic regression) is not invalidated.

**What I must concede:** The absolute performance estimates (AUROC, ECE) reported under this design will be optimistic relative to production deployment. The methodology does not establish whether the transformer generalizes forward in calendar time better than the baseline. A temporal holdout would answer a strictly more production-relevant question. This is a genuine scope limitation that should be stated explicitly in the paper, and ideally a temporal split should be run as a secondary analysis.

**Remaining uncertainty:** How large the optimism gap is depends on how much behavioral pattern drift occurred over the 24-month panel. In a stable marketplace, this may be small; in a period with major platform changes or macro shocks, it could be substantial.

---

### Critique 2: ECE as primary metric is poorly matched to the evaluation design and will be dominated by the majority class

**Critic's claim:** Global ECE over uniform decile bins in a class-imbalanced churn dataset (5–20% base rate) is dominated by the low-probability mass of non-churners. A model perfectly calibrated for non-churners but miscalibrated at the top risk decile will show excellent global ECE. The retention workflow cares specifically about the top-k risk tier, so global ECE masks exactly the failures that are most costly. Additionally, the transformer may produce sharper, overconfident predictions in the high-risk tier, appearing worse on global ECE than logistic regression even when it is operationally superior.

**Assessment:** This is correct. I concede this critique almost entirely.

The design intent behind ECE as primary metric was to emphasize calibration quality for budget-aware tiered intervention — the argument being that if predicted probabilities are well-calibrated, cost-sensitive decision policies can be applied directly. That intent is sound. But the execution is flawed: uniform decile bins in an imbalanced setting do not achieve that intent. Most of the decile mass will sit in the 0–20% predicted probability range, and calibration in that region is largely irrelevant to retention decisions. The bins that matter operationally — the 80th–100th percentile of predicted risk — will contain a small fraction of customers and will have negligible weight in the global ECE calculation.

The critic's additional point about transformer overconfidence is also well-taken. A transformer that achieves better AUROC but is overconfident in the top decile will show worse global ECE than a logistic regression that produces conservative, well-regularized probabilities everywhere. This could lead to the false conclusion that logistic regression is 'better' for the stated use case when in fact the transformer is more useful for retention targeting.

**What I can defend:** The general motivation for calibration as a primary metric in tiered intervention settings is correct. The framing problem is in the implementation of ECE, not in the choice to care about calibration.

**What I must concede:** Global decile-binned ECE should not be the primary metric. At minimum, calibration should be reported stratified by predicted-probability quartile, and a specific high-risk-tier ECE (top decile or top ventile) should be the operational calibration metric. A reliability diagram broken out by risk tier would make the distinction visible. As stated, the metric is misaligned with its stated purpose.

**Remaining uncertainty:** None material — the structural argument is sound and the fix is straightforward. This does not invalidate the overall experiment; it requires a metric adjustment.

---

### Critique 3: The baseline is too weak to isolate the benefit of sequential structure

**Critic's claim:** Logistic regression on 12 static features confounds two things: (a) sequential inductive bias vs. (b) richer feature set and more expressive model. A GBDT on an expanded temporal feature set (trend slope, inter-purchase interval variance, session frequency trajectory) would partially decouple these. If the transformer beats only logistic regression but not a feature-rich GBDT, the conclusion that 'sequence structure specifically drives gains' is unsupported.

**Assessment:** This is a valid methodological concern about the strength of the isolation claim, though the framing requires some nuance.

The critic is correct in the abstract: if the only comparison is transformer vs. logistic regression on 12 aggregated features, any gain is consistent with 'more capacity + more implicit features' rather than 'sequential inductive bias specifically.' The 12-feature logistic regression is a reasonable industry sanity-check baseline but is not sufficient to support the specific mechanistic claim that sequential structure is the driver.

However, I would note that the methodology as written does not claim to isolate the mechanism at the level of 'only sequential inductive bias.' The stated claim is: 'whether sequence structure drives gains beyond what aggregated features capture.' A GBDT on 12 features would partially address this; a GBDT on an expanded 200-feature set would address it more fully. The appropriate intermediate comparison is a gradient-boosted model on the same 12 static features — which would control for model capacity — before escalating to an expanded feature set.

The critic's proposed addition (GBDT on expanded hand-crafted temporal features) is the right direction, but I would frame it as: (1) add GBDT on same 12 features to isolate model capacity, and (2) add GBDT on expanded temporal features to isolate feature richness vs. sequential encoding. Both are needed to fully support the isolation claim.

**What I can defend:** For the purpose of the experiment as scoped ('does the transformer beat the industry-standard static baseline used in production today'), the 12-feature logistic regression may be the operationally relevant comparison. Many deployed churn systems are exactly this — logistic regression on aggregate features. Showing the transformer beats this has direct business relevance.

**What I must concede:** The broader claim that 'sequence structure specifically drives gains' cannot be established with only this baseline. The conclusion should be scoped to 'the transformer outperforms the current production-style static baseline' rather than 'sequential modeling is the mechanism.' To support the mechanism claim, at minimum a GBDT on the same feature set — and ideally an expanded temporal feature set — must be added.

**Remaining uncertainty:** Empirically, it is common in e-commerce churn literature that well-tuned GBDTs on hand-crafted temporal features match or exceed transformers. This is an open empirical question for this dataset.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Summarizing the Pass 1 analysis:

- **Issue 1:** Conceded as a genuine scope limitation. The evaluation regime does not match production deployment. The relative comparison is still valid, but absolute estimates are optimistic and the forward-generalization question is not answered. A temporal split is needed as a secondary analysis. This is a significant limitation.

- **Issue 2:** Conceded almost entirely. Global ECE over uniform decile bins is structurally misaligned with the stated operational goal. The fix is straightforward (tier-stratified calibration metrics) but as designed, the primary metric is unreliable for the stated purpose. This is a genuine flaw.

- **Issue 3:** Largely conceded. The baseline is insufficient to isolate the claimed mechanism. The conclusion must be scoped more narrowly, and a GBDT baseline is needed. This is a real methodological gap.

All three critiques identify genuine flaws — one affecting deployment validity (Issue 1), one affecting metric alignment (Issue 2), one affecting mechanism isolation (Issue 3). I cannot argue that the methodology is sound as stated for its full stated scope. However, Issues 2 and 3 are correctable through metric adjustments and baseline additions without redesigning the core experiment. Issue 1 requires a meaningful design change (temporal split as secondary analysis) and a scoped-down set of conclusions from the primary design. The core architecture comparison and confound controls are well-executed. These are not catastrophic failures but they are not minor quibbles either — taken together, they represent multiple critical limitations that the current design does not adequately handle.

The appropriate verdict is **critique_wins**: the critic has identified genuine critical flaws that I cannot adequately rebut within the current design as stated. The methodology is recoverable with modifications, but as written it cannot support its stated conclusions.
all_issues_raised: Random customer-level split across a 24-month panel does not enforce temporal holdout, allowing cross-customer temporal leakage and creating an evaluation regime that does not reflect production deployment where the model always predicts forward in time.
ECE over uniform decile bins in a class-imbalanced dataset is dominated by the non-churn majority and will mask miscalibration in the high-risk tier that the retention workflow actually depends on.

Logistic regression on 12 static features is an insufficient baseline to isolate the benefit of sequential structure; a GBDT on an expanded hand-crafted temporal feature set is needed to decouple sequence modeling from model capacity and feature richness.
all_issues_adjudicated: Random customer-level split across a 24-month panel creates a regime mismatch with production deployment where the model always predicts forward in time; the methodology does not establish forward temporal generalization, and absolute performance estimates will be optimistic—the defender concedes this as a genuine scope limitation requiring a temporal holdout as secondary analysis.

Global ECE over uniform decile bins in a class-imbalanced churn dataset is dominated by the non-churn majority and masks miscalibration in the high-risk tier that the retention workflow actually depends on; the defender concedes the primary metric is structurally misaligned with its stated operational purpose.

Logistic regression on 12 static features is insufficient to isolate the benefit of sequential structure from model capacity and feature richness; the defender concedes the mechanism claim cannot be supported without at minimum a GBDT baseline on the same and expanded feature sets.
